title: Today's AI Brief: Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them
summary: A look at the growing reliance on AI in coding and its potential implications.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them
imageCaption: Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them
imageSourceTitle: Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them

body.text: I'm looking at a fascinating trend in the world of coding. It seems that developers are now refusing to work without AI tools. According to a recent report on TechCrunch, researchers have found that in 2026, AI coding tools have become an integral part of developers' workflows. I guess you could say AI has become the ultimate coding sidekick. But what's interesting is that this reliance on AI might not be entirely positive.

While AI is undoubtedly speeding up the coding process, researchers are questioning whether it's actually improving the quality of the code. It's a bit like having a super-fast writer who can churn out novels in a day, but the grammar and plot might leave a lot to be desired. I'm not sure which one I'd prefer – speed or quality, that is. This debate reminds me of the early days of spell-checking tools, which were great for catching typos but sometimes led to creative workarounds that weren't exactly grammatically correct.

Coders are refusing to work without AI — and that could come back to bite them is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: In 2026, you cannot pry AI coding tools out of developers’ vise grip, researchers have discovered. But while AI is undoubtedly helping coders produce code faster, it may not be producing better code, other researchers warn. And that could cause problems down the road for them. The main content says: Railway , a San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending a dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in a Series B funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the limitations of legacy cloud infrastructure. My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
So that is the brief version: a few headlines, a little context, and just enough connective tissue to make the day easier to follow without turning the whole thing into homework.
